play.title: Don't leak PII, PHI, or secrets in error messages or logs
play.context: Sensitive data that enters exception messages or log metadata becomes searchable in observability systems by anyone with log access. Keeping PII, PHI, and secrets out of error handling protects veterans' data, prevents HIPAA exposure, and ensures leaked credentials cannot be replayed. A clean separation between error context and sensitive data lets the API maintain trust with consumers while giving SREs full diagnostic visibility.

rules.rule: Use safe identifiers (UUIDs, internal IDs, case IDs) in error messages and logs, never PII (names, emails, SSNs), PHI (medical info), or secrets (API keys, tokens).
Scrub sensitive data from stack traces and exception messages before they reach logging infrastructure.
Use allowlists for meta fields in structured logs; never blindly include user data or response bodies.
Apply DataScrubber for automatic PII/PHI redaction on any log payload that could contain user data.

investigate_before_answering.step: Read the full method to identify what data the response body contains. Determine if it is OAuth credentials, medical records (PHI), PII (SSN, DOB), or other sensitive data.
Identify all raise and log statements in the method that interpolate response bodies. Check for double-logging patterns (HTTP body + nested body).

**Check error response hash construction (MANDATORY).** Look for hash literals that include raw upstream data as values — not just string interpolation, but also direct method calls and `.try` in hash values. Common patterns: - `backend_response: error.try(:original_body)` — raw upstream body rendered to client in error meta - `original_error: error.try(:message)` — exception message that may contain upstream response text or PII - `vamf_body: @env.body` — raw upstream body stored in exception `response_values` that may flow through to `render json:` - `source: { vamf_body: body }` — raw body in exception source field
Trace the hash: does it reach `render json:` (client-facing) or only internal logs? If it reaches `render json:`, it is a finding. If the exception class stores it in `response_values` or `original_body`, check whether ExceptionHandling or a custom error handler renders those fields to the client.
Determine what safe identifiers are available in scope (case_id, uuid, request_id, status code) that can replace the response body for debugging.
Check if this pattern is copy-pasted across sibling API client files in the same module. Search for the same raise pattern in related files.
Verify whether a typed exception class already exists for this API client, or whether one needs to be created.

severity_assessment.critical: response body containing OAuth tokens, API keys, or credentials is logged or included in raise messages
response body containing PHI (medical records, diagnoses, treatments) is logged or included in raise messages
PII (SSN, DOB, address) is included in error messages or log meta fields

raw upstream response body (`.original_body`, `.body`, `vamf_body`) rendered to clients via `render json:` in error response meta or detail fields; upstream services may echo veteran data in error responses
severity_assessment.high: raw params hash is passed into log meta fields without allowlisting
the same leakage pattern is copy-pasted across multiple API client files
exception `.message` or `.response_values` rendered to clients without sanitization; BackendServiceException messages include the full response_values hash which may contain raw upstream body
severity_assessment.medium: response body logged for internal-only API with no user data
severity_assessment.false_positive: Sanitized or truncated response bodies where PII has been explicitly stripped before logging (e.g., allowlisted keys only, `body.slice(0, 100)` on known-safe payloads). Safe identifiers such as UUIDs, case IDs, ICNs, and form submission IDs are not PII; they are opaque handles with no personal information embedded. Also, response bodies logged at debug level behind a feature flag for temporary troubleshooting are lower risk than production error-path logging. Exception classes that sanitize `original_body` before storage (e.g., running it through an anonymizer or stripping to safe keys in the constructor) are not leaking raw data even if the field name sounds suspicious — read the exception class definition before flagging.

play.pr_comment_template:
**Don't leak PII, PHI, or secrets in error messages or logs** | `CRITICAL`
`{{file_path}}:{{line_number}}` -- `raise` interpolates `resp.body` which contains {{sensitive_data_type}}. This data is persisted in Datadog/Sentry logs where it is searchable and accessible to engineers.
**Why this matters:** {{specific_consequence}} (e.g., OAuth tokens in logs grant permanent API access; medical records in logs violate HIPAA).
**Suggested fix:** ```ruby unless resp.status == 200 Rails.logger.error('Token exchange failed', { status: resp.status }) raise TokenExchangeError, "Token exchange failed with status #{resp.status}" end ```
**Verify:** - [ ] No `resp.body` or `response.body` in raise/log statements - [ ] Error messages contain only safe identifiers (status code, case_id, uuid) - [ ] Sibling API client files checked for same pattern - [ ] Meta fields use allowlisted safe fields only
[Play: Don't leak PII, PHI, or secrets](01-dont-leak-pii-phi-secrets.xml)

example.name: Logging entire OAuth token response
example.bad: raise "response code: #{resp.status}, response body: #{resp.body}" unless resp.status == 200 # Logs entire OAuth token response body containing access_token, refresh_token, user profile
example.good: unless resp.status == 200 raise TokenExchangeError, "Token exchange failed with status #{resp.status}" # Logs only status code, not response body with credentials end

example.name: Logging CHAMPVA medical report response
example.bad: raise "response code: #{resp.status}, response body: #{resp.body}" unless resp.status == 200 # Logs entire CHAMPVA medical report response (PHI: diagnoses, treatments, prescriptions)
example.good: unless resp.status == 200 raise PegaApiError, "CHAMPVA report failed: HTTP #{resp.status}, case_id: #{case_id}" # Logs only status code + safe case_id, not PHI-laden response end

example.name: Double-logging nested response body
example.bad: unless response['statusCode'] == 200 raise "alternate response code: #{response['statusCode']}, response body: #{response['body']}" # Logs response body AGAIN, doubling PHI exposure end
example.good: unless response['statusCode'] == 200 raise PegaApiError, "CHAMPVA report internal error: status #{response['statusCode']}, case_id: #{case_id}" # Logs only status code, not nested response body with medical records end

example.name: Raw params in log metadata
example.bad: meta: { user_data: params } # params can contain SSNs, DOBs, medical data
example.good: meta: { case_id: case_id, status: resp.status } # Only safe identifiers in log metadata

example.name: Raw upstream body rendered to client in error response meta
Exception class stores raw body in response_values
